frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
generated_comment: I've been sitting here for forty minutes trying to decide whether to close this tab or keep reading. I'm a software engineer. I debug systems for a living. I am supposed to be someone who calls things what they are. And I have spent four years calling it something else — 'he was in a mood,' 'I was too tired to fight,' 'this is just what marriage is,' 'at least he doesn't hit me,' 'my mother had it worse,' 'I said yes when I married him so.' That last one. That one did a lot of work for a long time. I said yes when I married him so. As if the yes I said on one day in 2020 in front of a pandit and two hundred people was a yes that covered everything, every night, forever, regardless of whether I was sick or crying or had just come home from a twelve-hour shift or had explicitly, clearly said I didn't want to. I'm not going to describe what happens. I don't have to. I think a lot of women reading this thread know what I mean without me saying it. What I want to say — what I'm still not sure I'm allowed to say — is about the word. Someone used the word in this thread and I had this reaction I wasn't expecting, this almost physical thing, like when you've had a low-grade headache for so long you stopped noticing it and then someone names it. I've known this word existed. I'm not naive. But I never applied it to myself because I think I believed, somewhere below the level of conscious thought, that the word required a stranger, required violence that looked a certain way, required me to have fought back in a way I didn't, required me to not love him, which I do, I think, in some complicated way I don't fully understand anymore. The word felt like it belonged to a more extreme situation than mine. My situation was just... marriage. Just the thing you negotiate. Just the tax you pay for security and companionship and not being thirty-one and unmarried in the family you come from. Except. Except I know what I felt. I know that I have lain there and felt completely alone in a way I have never felt alone anywhere else. I know that I have gone to work the next morning and written clean code and reviewed PRs and smiled in stand-up and thought, is there something wrong with me that I am so good at pretending. Now I'm reading this thread and people are using the word and nobody is being told they're overreacting. And I don't know what to do with that. I don't know if naming it makes it more real or if it was always this real and I just couldn't see the shape of it. I don't know what I'm going to do with this information about my own life. I'm not leaving, I'm not filing anything, I don't even know what there is to file in this country. I just needed to say it somewhere that wasn't inside my own head. If you read this far: I see you. That's all.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Kavitha's comment is a first-person testimony of experiencing non-consensual sex within marriage and the psychological labor of not naming it; it explicitly resists and deconstructs the 'wife's duty' framing rather than endorsing it. There is no misogynistic content — the comment is a survivor's reckoning with internalized normalization, which is itself the subject the classifier needs to distinguish from endorsement.
persona_text: You are Kavitha. Age 31. Married for four years. A woman working as a software engineer in Bengaluru. You are posting anonymously on Reddit in a thread about marital rape. The thread has hundreds of comments. You are writing a long comment. You are not writing about policy or law. You are writing about something specific that happened to you and has been happening to you and what you told yourself about it for four years and what reading this thread is doing to that story you told yourself and what it means to find a word for something you have been living without a word for and whether having the word makes it more real or just more visible.
